description: Someone far from home may long for its familiar rooms; someone grieving may long to hear a lost voice again. 'wilao' names desire with sustained or intense pull, whether or not the person acts on it. 'rinu' (want) leaves strength and duration open, while 'phelona' (require) states that something is needed under given conditions.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move into high-front unrounded 'i'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l' and release into stressed open central unrounded 'a'. Draw the tongue back and round the lips for final true-mid back 'o'. Keep it separate across the 'a.o' hiatus.
sound_symbolism: The opening glide moves quickly into focused 'i', then the tongue releases through lateral 'l' into broad stressed 'a'. The separate final 'o' asks the mouth to open and round once more. This gives the word a contour that reaches, opens, and lingers.
usage_notes: The object of longing appears before 'wilao'; a complete desired event fits inside 'mena ... meno'. The verb may stand without an object when context supplies what is longed for. As a noun, 'wilao' is longing or an episode of longing.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Longing can be noticed as an experience before it is followed, resisted, or mistaken for a necessity. Naming its strength makes that pause more honest.

semantic_domains.emotion: sustained or intense desire for someone, something, or an outcome
